title: Covenant
description: The binding agreements through which God relates to his people. From Noah's rainbow to Abraham's promise to Moses at Sinai to David's throne to the new covenant in Christ's blood, covenant is the structural backbone of biblical theology.

journey_stops.label: Noahic Covenant
journey_stops.development: First covenant: universal scope, unilateral, with all creation. No conditions imposed on humanity.
journey_stops.what_changes: God binds himself never to destroy by flood again. The rainbow serves as the covenant sign visible to both parties.

journey_stops.label: Abrahamic Covenant
journey_stops.development: Narrows from universal to one family. Still unilateral: God alone passes between the severed pieces.
journey_stops.what_changes: Land, offspring, and blessing are promised. The covenant family that will mediate blessing to all nations begins here.

journey_stops.label: Covenant Sign: Circumcision
journey_stops.development: The Abrahamic covenant receives a bodily sign. Every male bears the mark of belonging to the covenant community.
journey_stops.what_changes: Covenant identity becomes physically inscribed. The sign distinguishes Abraham's line from the nations.

journey_stops.label: Sinai Covenant Proposal
journey_stops.development: Shifts from family to nation. Bilateral: 'if you obey... you will be my treasured possession.' Conditions are introduced.
journey_stops.what_changes: Israel becomes a 'kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' National identity is now covenantal identity.

journey_stops.label: Covenant Ratification by Blood
journey_stops.development: The Sinai covenant is ratified with sacrificial blood sprinkled on both altar and people.
journey_stops.what_changes: Blood becomes the medium of covenant ratification, establishing a pattern that persists through the entire sacrificial system.

journey_stops.label: Davidic Covenant
journey_stops.development: Narrows further: one family within the nation. Unconditional promise of an eternal dynasty.
journey_stops.what_changes: The throne of David is promised permanence. Messianic expectation is anchored to a specific royal line.

journey_stops.label: New Covenant Promised
journey_stops.development: Declares the Mosaic covenant broken and announces a new one. Law will be internalized, not externally imposed.
journey_stops.what_changes: The problem of covenant-breaking is addressed at its root: the human heart. Universal knowledge of God is promised.

journey_stops.label: Spirit and New Heart
journey_stops.development: Expands the new covenant vision: God will replace hearts of stone with hearts of flesh and put his Spirit within his people.
journey_stops.what_changes: The mechanism of covenant obedience shifts from external law to internal transformation by the Spirit.

journey_stops.label: New Covenant in Christ's Blood
journey_stops.development: Jesus identifies the cup as 'the new covenant in my blood.' All prior covenants converge in his sacrificial death.
journey_stops.what_changes: The new covenant is inaugurated. The pattern of blood ratification from Exodus 24 reaches its fulfillment.

journey_stops.label: Better Covenant, Better Mediator
journey_stops.development: Systematic argument that the new covenant is superior to the old. Christ is mediator of a 'better covenant, enacted on better promises.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The old covenant is declared 'obsolete.' The new covenant is shown to fulfill, not merely replace, what preceded it.

title: Sacrifice & Atonement
description: The system of blood offerings that addresses human sin and restores relationship with a holy God. From Abel's firstborn lamb through the Levitical system to Christ's once-for-all sacrifice, atonement reveals both the gravity of sin and the depths of divine love.

journey_stops.label: First Animal Covering
journey_stops.development: God provides garments of skin for Adam and Eve after the fall. An animal dies to cover human shame.
journey_stops.what_changes: The pattern of substitutionary covering is introduced. Sin requires a death to address its consequences.

journey_stops.label: The Binding of Isaac
journey_stops.development: Abraham is tested to offer Isaac, but God provides a ram as substitute. 'On the mountain of the LORD it shall be provided.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The principle of divine provision of the sacrifice is established. God himself will supply what he demands.

journey_stops.label: Passover Lamb
journey_stops.development: A lamb without blemish is slain; its blood on the doorposts causes the destroyer to pass over. Deliverance through substitutionary blood.
journey_stops.what_changes: Sacrifice becomes communal and commemorative. The annual Passover ritualizes Israel's foundational experience of redemption through blood.

journey_stops.label: Day of Atonement
journey_stops.development: The high priest enters the Most Holy Place once a year with blood for the sins of the nation. The scapegoat carries sins away.
journey_stops.what_changes: National atonement is systematized. Two goats illustrate two aspects: propitiation (blood sprinkled) and expiation (sins removed).

journey_stops.label: Blood is the Life
journey_stops.development: The theological rationale for blood sacrifice is stated explicitly: 'The life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Sacrifice is grounded in theology, not mere ritual. Blood represents life given in place of life forfeited.

journey_stops.label: Suffering Servant
journey_stops.development: The Servant bears the sins of many, is pierced for transgressions, crushed for iniquities. His suffering is vicarious and voluntary.
journey_stops.what_changes: Sacrifice becomes personal and prophetic. A single figure, not an animal, will accomplish what the entire sacrificial system pointed toward.

journey_stops.label: Lamb of God
journey_stops.development: John the Baptist identifies Jesus as 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.' All prior sacrificial imagery converges.
journey_stops.what_changes: The sacrifice is no longer prospective but present. The one to whom all lambs pointed has arrived.

journey_stops.label: Once for All
journey_stops.development: Christ entered the heavenly sanctuary 'not with the blood of goats and calves but with his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The repetitive cycle of animal sacrifice ends. A single, unrepeatable sacrifice accomplishes what countless offerings could only anticipate.

title: Kingship & Kingdom
description: God's sovereign rule over all creation, delegated through human kings and ultimately embodied in the Messiah. The tension between human kingship and divine kingship drives Israel's history from judges to exile to the coming King of kings.

journey_stops.label: Scepter from Judah
journey_stops.development: Jacob's deathbed oracle assigns royal destiny to Judah: 'The scepter shall not depart from Judah... until tribute comes to him.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Kingship is linked to a specific tribe. The royal line is narrowed before Israel even becomes a nation.

journey_stops.label: The Law of the King
journey_stops.development: Moses anticipates monarchy and provides constitutional limits: the king must not multiply horses, wives, or silver, and must write out the Torah.
journey_stops.what_changes: Israelite kingship is defined as fundamentally different from pagan models. The king is under the law, not above it.

journey_stops.label: Israel Demands a King
journey_stops.development: Israel requests a king 'like all the nations.' God tells Samuel they have rejected divine kingship, but permits the request.
journey_stops.what_changes: The tension between divine and human kingship becomes explicit. Human monarchy is both concession and vehicle for God's purposes.

journey_stops.label: Davidic Covenant
journey_stops.development: God promises David an eternal dynasty. His son will build the temple, and the throne will be established forever.
journey_stops.what_changes: Kingship becomes covenantal. The Davidic line carries the weight of messianic expectation from this point forward.

journey_stops.label: Kingdom Torn
journey_stops.development: Solomon's idolatry triggers the division of the kingdom. Yet God preserves one tribe for David's sake.
journey_stops.what_changes: The failure of human kingship begins. Even the wisest king falls, yet the Davidic promise is not annulled.

journey_stops.label: Ideal King Prophesied
journey_stops.development: A child will sit on David's throne with the government on his shoulders. His titles exceed any human monarch: Mighty God, Prince of Peace.
journey_stops.what_changes: The messianic king is described in terms that transcend human categories. Divine and human kingship converge in one figure.

journey_stops.label: Son of Man Receives Dominion
journey_stops.development: One 'like a son of man' approaches the Ancient of Days and receives universal, everlasting dominion over all peoples and nations.
journey_stops.what_changes: The kingdom vision becomes cosmic. The coming king's reign is not merely Israelite but universal and eternal.

journey_stops.label: Triumphal Entry
journey_stops.development: Jesus enters Jerusalem on a donkey, fulfilling Zechariah 9:9. The crowds proclaim him 'Son of David.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The messianic king arrives, but on a donkey, not a war horse. Kingship is redefined through humility.

journey_stops.label: King of Kings
journey_stops.development: Christ returns as conquering king, wearing many crowns, with 'King of Kings and Lord of Lords' written on his robe.
journey_stops.what_changes: All royal imagery reaches its climax. The humble king of the triumphal entry is revealed as the sovereign ruler of all creation.

title: Holiness
description: The 'otherness' of God that sets him apart from all creation, and the call for his people to reflect that separateness. Holiness is not merely moral purity but the very nature of the divine presence that both attracts and terrifies.

journey_stops.label: Holy Ground
journey_stops.development: Moses encounters the burning bush. God commands: 'Take off your sandals, for the place where you are standing is holy ground.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Holiness is revealed as the fundamental attribute of God's presence. It creates a boundary that requires human response.

journey_stops.label: Holy Mountain
journey_stops.development: Mount Sinai is consecrated; boundaries are set. Touching the mountain means death. The people must consecrate themselves.
journey_stops.what_changes: Holiness is shown to be dangerous to the unprepared. Separation and consecration become prerequisites for approaching God.

journey_stops.label: Be Holy, for I Am Holy
journey_stops.development: The central command of Leviticus: Israel's holiness is to mirror God's holiness. Purity laws are the practical outworking.
journey_stops.what_changes: Holiness becomes a calling, not just a divine attribute. Israel's entire way of life — food, clothing, agriculture — is shaped by the holiness imperative.

journey_stops.label: Holy, Holy, Holy
journey_stops.development: Isaiah sees the LORD in the temple; seraphim cry 'Holy, holy, holy.' The prophet is undone: 'I am a man of unclean lips.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The threefold repetition intensifies the concept to its absolute degree. Holiness produces both terror and cleansing.

journey_stops.label: Sanctifying the Name
journey_stops.development: God will vindicate his holy name, profaned among the nations through Israel's exile. Restoration serves his holiness, not Israel's merit.
journey_stops.what_changes: Holiness becomes the driving motive of redemptive history. God acts 'for the sake of my holy name,' not for human deserving.

journey_stops.label: Be Perfect
journey_stops.development: Jesus reframes the holiness command: 'Be perfect, as your heavenly Father is perfect.' The standard is not merely ritual purity but character.
journey_stops.what_changes: Holiness is internalized. External purity codes give way to holiness of heart and motive.

journey_stops.label: Holy in All Conduct
journey_stops.development: Peter quotes Leviticus 11:44 to the church: 'Be holy in all your conduct.' The Levitical call extends to all believers.
journey_stops.what_changes: The holiness imperative crosses the covenant boundary. What was Israel's distinctive calling becomes the universal mark of God's people.

journey_stops.label: Eternal Holy, Holy, Holy
journey_stops.development: The four living creatures ceaselessly declare 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty.' Isaiah's vision becomes eternal reality.
journey_stops.what_changes: Holiness is revealed as the eternal atmosphere of heaven. The story that began at the burning bush culminates in ceaseless worship.

title: Exile & Return
description: The pattern of displacement and restoration that runs from Eden through Babylon and beyond. Exile is both judgment and refining; return is both geographical and spiritual, pointing to the ultimate homecoming in the new creation.

journey_stops.label: Exile from Eden
journey_stops.development: Adam and Eve are driven from the garden; cherubim and a flaming sword guard the way to the tree of life.
journey_stops.what_changes: The primal exile establishes the pattern: sin leads to separation from God's presence. All subsequent exiles echo this first expulsion.

journey_stops.label: Abraham's Call from Exile
journey_stops.development: God calls Abraham out of Ur to a promised land. The journey from exile to homeland begins with a single family.
journey_stops.what_changes: Return from exile becomes linked to promise and faith. The land is both gift and destination.

journey_stops.label: Exodus from Egypt
journey_stops.development: Israel crosses the Red Sea, escaping 400 years of Egyptian bondage. The paradigmatic deliverance event of the Old Testament.
journey_stops.what_changes: Exodus becomes the controlling metaphor for all future deliverances. Every return from exile is a 'new exodus.'

journey_stops.label: Northern Exile to Assyria
journey_stops.development: The ten northern tribes are deported to Assyria in 722 BC. The text gives a theological autopsy: they sinned, were warned, refused to listen.
journey_stops.what_changes: Exile is shown to be covenant consequence, not arbitrary punishment. The prophetic warnings are vindicated.

journey_stops.label: Babylonian Exile
journey_stops.development: Jerusalem falls in 586 BC. The temple is burned, the walls broken down, the people deported. The Davidic dynasty is interrupted.
journey_stops.what_changes: The unthinkable happens: God's own house is destroyed. Every theological certainty — temple, monarchy, land — is removed.

journey_stops.label: Comfort, Comfort My People
journey_stops.development: The voice cries: 'In the wilderness prepare the way of the LORD.' A new exodus through the desert is announced.
journey_stops.what_changes: The exile is not the final word. God's return to his people is described in language that echoes and surpasses the first exodus.

journey_stops.label: Cyrus Decree — Return Begins
journey_stops.development: Cyrus of Persia issues the decree permitting Jews to return and rebuild the temple. Jeremiah's 70-year prophecy is fulfilled.
journey_stops.what_changes: Physical return begins, but the spiritual restoration promised by the prophets remains incomplete. The second temple lacks the glory of the first.

journey_stops.label: The Prodigal Returns
journey_stops.development: Jesus tells of a son who squanders his inheritance in a 'far country' and returns to his father's embrace. Israel's exile story in miniature.
journey_stops.what_changes: Exile and return become personal and universal. The father's welcome redefines what homecoming means: not merit but grace.

title: Wisdom
description: The skill for living that begins with the fear of the LORD. Biblical wisdom is not abstract philosophy but practical knowledge rooted in covenant relationship — knowing how to navigate life in alignment with the Creator's design.

journey_stops.label: Fear of the LORD
journey_stops.development: The programmatic statement of Israel's wisdom tradition: 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Wisdom is anchored in theology, not mere pragmatism. True knowledge begins with right relationship to God.

journey_stops.label: Wisdom Personified at Creation
journey_stops.development: Wisdom speaks as a person present at creation: 'Before the mountains were settled... I was there.' She was 'beside him, like a master workman.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Wisdom is elevated from practical skill to cosmic principle. The created order itself embodies divine wisdom.

journey_stops.label: Where Is Wisdom Found?
journey_stops.development: A hymn to wisdom's inaccessibility. No human effort can discover it; 'God understands the way to it.' The conclusion: fear the LORD.
journey_stops.what_changes: The limits of human wisdom are exposed. True wisdom is not achievable by human striving but received as divine gift.

journey_stops.label: Vanity of Vanities
journey_stops.development: Qoheleth declares all things 'hevel' — vapor, breath, meaningless under the sun. The wisdom tradition confronts its own limits.
journey_stops.what_changes: Wisdom literature gains its necessary counterpoint. Conventional wisdom cannot explain all of life's inequities.

journey_stops.label: The Whole Duty of Humanity
journey_stops.development: After exhaustive investigation, the conclusion: 'Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Skeptical wisdom circles back to faith. Even the most rigorous questioning ends at the same starting point as Proverbs 1:7.

journey_stops.label: Greater Than Solomon
journey_stops.development: Jesus declares that 'something greater than Solomon is here.' The queen of the South came for Solomon's wisdom; a greater wisdom is now present.
journey_stops.what_changes: Wisdom is no longer merely a tradition or a body of teaching but a person. Christological wisdom supersedes sapiential wisdom.

journey_stops.label: Foolishness of God
journey_stops.development: The cross is 'foolishness to those who are perishing' but 'the power of God' to those being saved. God's wisdom inverts human categories.
journey_stops.what_changes: The cross redefines wisdom entirely. What appears as ultimate folly — a crucified Messiah — is revealed as ultimate wisdom.

journey_stops.label: All Treasures of Wisdom in Christ
journey_stops.development: In Christ 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.' Proverbs 8's personified Wisdom finds its ultimate referent.
journey_stops.what_changes: The entire wisdom tradition is christologically fulfilled. The search for wisdom ends in a person, not a principle.

title: Spirit of God
description: The divine presence and power at work from creation's first breath to Pentecost's flames. The Spirit empowers prophets and kings, gives life to dry bones, indwells believers, and guarantees the coming resurrection.

journey_stops.label: Spirit over the Waters
journey_stops.development: The Spirit of God hovers over the face of the deep. The first act of creation begins with the Spirit's brooding presence.
journey_stops.what_changes: The Spirit is associated with creation and the bringing of order from chaos. Creative power is pneumatological from the beginning.

journey_stops.label: Spirit upon the Judges
journey_stops.development: The Spirit of the LORD comes upon Othniel, and he judges Israel. A recurring pattern: the Spirit empowers specific leaders for specific tasks.
journey_stops.what_changes: The Spirit's work becomes charismatic — given for a task, then withdrawn. Empowerment is temporary and functional.

journey_stops.label: Spirit Transferred to David
journey_stops.development: Samuel anoints David; the Spirit rushes upon him 'from that day forward.' Simultaneously, the Spirit departs from Saul.
journey_stops.what_changes: The Spirit is linked to anointing and kingship. The transfer marks divine election and rejection.

journey_stops.label: Spirit upon the Branch
journey_stops.development: The coming Davidic ruler will be endowed with the Spirit in fullness: wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, fear of the LORD.
journey_stops.what_changes: The messianic king will not merely receive the Spirit temporarily but will be permanently characterized by the Spirit's sevenfold endowment.

journey_stops.label: Valley of Dry Bones
journey_stops.development: The Spirit breathes life into dead bones. National resurrection is accomplished through the Spirit's creative, life-giving power.
journey_stops.what_changes: The Spirit's role expands from individual empowerment to corporate, national resurrection. The Spirit recreates what death has destroyed.

journey_stops.label: Spirit Poured Out on All Flesh
journey_stops.development: God promises to pour out his Spirit on all flesh — sons, daughters, old, young, servants. No social or gender limitation.
journey_stops.what_changes: The Spirit's work will no longer be restricted to select leaders. Universal outpouring replaces occasional charismatic endowment.

journey_stops.label: Pentecost
journey_stops.development: The Spirit descends as tongues of fire. All present are filled. Peter identifies this as Joel's prophecy fulfilled.
journey_stops.what_changes: The age of the Spirit begins. What was promised through the prophets becomes the permanent, universal experience of the church.

journey_stops.label: Spirit of Life in Christ Jesus
journey_stops.development: Paul declares that anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him. The Spirit who raised Jesus will also give life to mortal bodies.
journey_stops.what_changes: The Spirit becomes the defining mark of belonging to Christ. Indwelling, not occasional empowerment, is the new normal.

title: Faith
description: Trust in God that takes him at his word. Abraham believed and it was credited as righteousness; the righteous live by faith. Biblical faith is not blind assent but confident reliance on the character and promises of God.

journey_stops.label: Abraham Believed
journey_stops.development: Abraham 'believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness.' The paradigmatic act of faith in the Hebrew Bible.
journey_stops.what_changes: Faith is established as the basis of right standing with God. Righteousness is credited, not earned.

journey_stops.label: Israel Believed at the Sea
journey_stops.development: After the Red Sea crossing, 'the people feared the LORD, and they believed in the LORD and in his servant Moses.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Faith becomes communal and responsive to divine action. The pattern is established: God acts, the people believe.

journey_stops.label: The Righteous Shall Live by Faith
journey_stops.development: In the face of Babylonian invasion and apparent divine silence, the prophet receives the foundational principle: faithfulness is the path of life.
journey_stops.what_changes: Faith is defined against the backdrop of crisis. Trust in God persists even when circumstances contradict the promises.

journey_stops.label: Centurion's Faith
journey_stops.development: A Gentile centurion demonstrates faith that amazes Jesus: 'I have not found such great faith even in Israel.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Faith crosses ethnic boundaries. The expected people lack what an outsider possesses, foreshadowing the Gentile mission.

journey_stops.label: Help My Unbelief
journey_stops.development: A desperate father cries: 'I believe; help my unbelief!' Jesus heals his son despite the father's mixed faith.
journey_stops.what_changes: Faith is shown to be compatible with doubt. Honest struggling faith, not perfect certainty, is sufficient.

journey_stops.label: Faith of Abraham Revisited
journey_stops.development: Paul holds up Abraham as the exemplar of justifying faith: 'against hope he believed in hope.' His faith was counted as righteousness.
journey_stops.what_changes: Genesis 15:6 becomes the cornerstone of Pauline soteriology. Faith, not works of the law, is the instrument of justification.

journey_stops.label: Faith Defined
journey_stops.development: Faith is 'the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.' The great catalog of faith heroes follows.
journey_stops.what_changes: Faith receives its most concentrated theological definition. It bridges the gap between promise and fulfillment, visible and invisible.

journey_stops.label: Faith and Works
journey_stops.development: James insists that 'faith without works is dead.' Abraham's faith was 'completed by his works' when he offered Isaac.
journey_stops.what_changes: The faith tradition receives its necessary complement. Genuine faith produces visible action; mere intellectual assent is insufficient.

title: Mercy & Grace
description: God's undeserved favor and compassion toward sinners. Mercy withholds deserved punishment; grace gives undeserved blessing. Together they reveal a God who delights to forgive and restore the broken.

journey_stops.label: The LORD, Merciful and Gracious
journey_stops.development: God proclaims his own name: 'merciful and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness.' The definitive self-revelation.
journey_stops.what_changes: God's character is revealed as fundamentally merciful. This formula becomes the most frequently quoted creed in the Old Testament.

journey_stops.label: Pardon in the Wilderness
journey_stops.development: After the golden calf rebellion and the spies' report, Moses appeals to Exodus 34:6. God pardons, but with consequences.
journey_stops.what_changes: Mercy is shown to coexist with judgment. Pardon does not eliminate consequences but preserves the covenant relationship.

journey_stops.label: David Pardoned
journey_stops.development: After David's confession of adultery and murder, Nathan declares: 'The LORD has put away your sin; you shall not die.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Grace reaches into the most grievous moral failure. Even the king who should die by his own law receives mercy.

journey_stops.label: As a Father Shows Compassion
journey_stops.development: Echoing Exodus 34:6: 'As far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions.' The image of a tender father.
journey_stops.what_changes: Mercy becomes intimate and parental. God's compassion is grounded in his knowledge of human frailty: 'He knows our frame.'

journey_stops.label: How Can I Give You Up?
journey_stops.development: God agonizes over Israel: 'My heart recoils within me; my compassion grows warm and tender.' Divine love wrestles with divine justice.
journey_stops.what_changes: Grace is revealed as costly to God himself. Mercy is not indifference to sin but the triumph of love over just wrath.

journey_stops.label: Grace to the Enemy
journey_stops.development: Jonah quotes Exodus 34:6 as a complaint: God's mercy extends even to Israel's worst enemy, Nineveh.
journey_stops.what_changes: Grace shatters national boundaries. The prophet who knows God's character resents its implications for the ungodly.

journey_stops.label: Seeking the Lost
journey_stops.development: Jesus tells of a shepherd who leaves ninety-nine sheep to find the one that is lost. Heaven rejoices over one sinner who repents.
journey_stops.what_changes: Grace becomes active and seeking. God does not merely wait for the sinner's return but pursues the lost.

journey_stops.label: By Grace Through Faith
journey_stops.development: Paul gives the definitive statement: 'By grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Grace is defined as the entire mechanism of salvation. Human contribution is excluded; even faith itself is gift.

title: Judgment
description: God's righteous evaluation and response to human sin. Judgment is not arbitrary anger but the necessary consequence of rebellion against a holy God. Yet even in judgment, God's aim is often redemptive — calling his people back through discipline.

journey_stops.label: The Flood
journey_stops.development: God sees that human wickedness is great and 'every intention of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually.' He resolves to destroy.
journey_stops.what_changes: The first comprehensive judgment establishes the pattern: divine patience has limits, and persistent evil calls forth devastating response.

journey_stops.label: Sodom and Gomorrah
journey_stops.development: Fire and sulfur rain from heaven, overthrowing the cities of the plain. Even Abraham's intercession could not find ten righteous.
journey_stops.what_changes: Judgment becomes localized and specific. Particular communities bear the consequences of their particular sins.

journey_stops.label: The Ten Plagues
journey_stops.development: Systematic judgments fall on Egypt, each targeting an Egyptian deity. Pharaoh's hardened heart escalates the severity.
journey_stops.what_changes: Judgment serves liberation. The plagues are not purposeless wrath but targeted acts that accomplish redemption.

journey_stops.label: Day of the LORD — Darkness
journey_stops.development: Israel expected the Day of the LORD as vindication. Amos inverts it: 'It is darkness, and not light.' God demands justice, not empty worship.
journey_stops.what_changes: Judgment turns inward toward God's own people. Covenant privilege does not exempt from covenant accountability.

journey_stops.label: Assyria as God's Rod
journey_stops.development: God uses Assyria as 'the rod of my anger' against Israel, then judges Assyria for its arrogance. Even instruments of judgment are accountable.
journey_stops.what_changes: Divine sovereignty encompasses pagan empires. God's use of nations does not excuse their moral culpability.

journey_stops.label: Seventy Years of Exile
journey_stops.development: Judah's refusal to heed prophetic warning results in 70 years of Babylonian exile. The covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28 are enacted.
journey_stops.what_changes: Judgment is shown to be neither arbitrary nor disproportionate. The covenant spelled out the consequences in advance; judgment is covenant enforcement.

journey_stops.label: Sheep and Goats
journey_stops.development: The Son of Man separates the nations. The criterion is not ritual observance but treatment of 'the least of these.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Final judgment is christological and ethical. Care for the vulnerable is care for Christ himself; neglect is rejection of him.

journey_stops.label: Great White Throne
journey_stops.development: The dead stand before the great white throne. Books are opened. Anyone not found in the book of life is cast into the lake of fire.
journey_stops.what_changes: Judgment reaches its absolute and final form. All human history stands before the throne; the distinction between the righteous and the wicked is made permanent.

title: Creation & New Creation
description: God's sovereign act of bringing all things into existence, and his promise to make all things new. The first creation groans under the curse; the new creation awaits the revealing of the sons of God. What was lost in Eden will be restored and surpassed.

journey_stops.label: Creation in Seven Days
journey_stops.development: God creates by speaking. Light, sky, land, vegetation, luminaries, creatures, and humanity emerge in ordered sequence. 'It was very good.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The world is established as purposeful, ordered, and good. God is sovereign over all that exists; nothing rivals him.

journey_stops.label: Humanity Formed from Dust
journey_stops.development: God forms adam from the adamah, breathes life into his nostrils. Human dignity and creaturely dependence are simultaneously established.
journey_stops.what_changes: Humanity is distinct: made of earth yet animated by divine breath. Image-bearing and dust-origin coexist.

journey_stops.label: God Answers from the Whirlwind
journey_stops.development: God challenges Job: 'Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth?' Creation's mystery silences human complaint.
journey_stops.what_changes: Creation serves as theodicy. The incomprehensibility of the created order mirrors the incomprehensibility of divine governance.

journey_stops.label: Heavens Declare God's Glory
journey_stops.development: The firmament speaks without words. Day pours forth speech to day; night reveals knowledge to night. Creation is revelation.
journey_stops.what_changes: The created order becomes a mode of divine communication. General revelation through nature complements special revelation through Torah.

journey_stops.label: New Heavens and New Earth
journey_stops.development: God declares: 'Behold, I create new heavens and a new earth.' The first creation will be surpassed, not merely restored.
journey_stops.what_changes: Creation theology gains an eschatological dimension. The original 'very good' will be exceeded by a new creation free from curse.

journey_stops.label: All Things through the Word
journey_stops.development: The Word who was with God and was God is identified as the agent of creation: 'All things were made through him.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Creation is christologically grounded. The one who will become incarnate is the same one through whom all things came into being.

journey_stops.label: Creation Groans
journey_stops.development: The whole creation 'has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth.' It waits with eager longing for the redemption of God's children.
journey_stops.what_changes: Creation is not static but participates in the drama of redemption. The fall affected the entire created order; redemption will restore it.

journey_stops.label: New Creation Realized
journey_stops.development: A new heaven and new earth appear. God dwells with humanity; death, mourning, and pain are no more. 'Behold, I am making all things new.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The arc from Genesis 1 to Revelation 21 is completed. Creation's trajectory is not dissolution but transformation and consummation.

title: Temple & Divine Presence
description: The place where heaven meets earth and God dwells among his people. From Eden as the first sanctuary through tabernacle and temple to Christ as the true temple to the church as living stones, God's presence defines sacred space.

journey_stops.label: Bethel — House of God
journey_stops.development: Jacob awakens from his ladder vision: 'Surely the LORD is in this place, and I did not know it.' He names it Bethel — house of God.
journey_stops.what_changes: Sacred space is first experienced as surprising encounter. God's presence is localized but unpredictable.

journey_stops.label: Tabernacle Blueprint
journey_stops.development: God commands: 'Let them make me a sanctuary, that I may dwell in their midst.' The tabernacle is built according to a heavenly pattern.
journey_stops.what_changes: God's presence becomes architecturally structured. The tabernacle is a portable Eden, mediating divine presence within the camp.

journey_stops.label: Glory Fills the Tabernacle
journey_stops.development: The cloud covers the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD fills the tabernacle. Even Moses cannot enter.
journey_stops.what_changes: The promise of Exodus 25:8 is fulfilled. God's kavod — his weighty, luminous presence — takes up residence among his people.

journey_stops.label: Glory Fills Solomon's Temple
journey_stops.development: The cloud fills the temple so that the priests cannot stand to minister. Solomon dedicates the house with prayer and sacrifice.
journey_stops.what_changes: The tabernacle's portability gives way to permanence. Jerusalem becomes the fixed center of God's earthly presence.

journey_stops.label: Glory Departs
journey_stops.development: Ezekiel watches the glory of the LORD leave the temple threshold and ascend from the city. The ichabod moment of Israel's history.
journey_stops.what_changes: The temple without the glory is merely a building. God's presence is shown to be free, not captive to a structure.

journey_stops.label: The Word Became Flesh
journey_stops.development: The Word 'tabernacled' (eskenosen) among us, 'and we have seen his glory.' The incarnation is the new temple.
journey_stops.what_changes: God's presence is no longer mediated through a building but through a person. Jesus himself is the locus of divine glory.

journey_stops.label: Your Body Is a Temple
journey_stops.development: Paul declares that the believer's body is 'a temple of the Holy Spirit.' The temple concept is radically individualized.
journey_stops.what_changes: Every believer becomes a sacred space. The Spirit who filled tabernacle and temple now indwells each member of Christ's body.

journey_stops.label: No Temple Needed
journey_stops.development: The new Jerusalem has no temple, 'for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb.' The mediation of sacred space is transcended.
journey_stops.what_changes: The temple trajectory reaches its telos: unmediated presence. The building that pointed to the person gives way to the person himself.

title: Redemption
description: The act of buying back what was lost or enslaved. Israel was redeemed from Egypt; the kinsman-redeemer restored family land; Christ redeems humanity from bondage to sin and death. The price is always costly — ultimately, the blood of the Lamb.

journey_stops.label: I Will Redeem You
journey_stops.development: God uses four 'I will' declarations to Moses, including 'I will redeem you with an outstretched arm.' The root ga'al — kinsman-redeemer — is invoked.
journey_stops.what_changes: Redemption is first defined in terms of deliverance from slavery. God acts as kinsman-redeemer for an enslaved people.

journey_stops.label: Boaz the Kinsman-Redeemer
journey_stops.development: Boaz exercises his right as go'el, redeeming Naomi's land and taking Ruth as wife. Law, love, and lineage converge.
journey_stops.what_changes: Redemption takes on personal and relational dimensions. The redeemer acts out of loyalty and love, not mere legal obligation.

journey_stops.label: I Have Redeemed You
journey_stops.development: God declares: 'Fear not, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by name, you are mine.' A new exodus is promised.
journey_stops.what_changes: Redemption becomes intimate and named. God redeems not a faceless mass but individuals known by name.

journey_stops.label: Redeemed Without Money
journey_stops.development: God will redeem Israel 'without money.' No purchase price is paid to Babylon; God's sovereign power alone effects the release.
journey_stops.what_changes: Redemption is shown to be purely gracious. No exchange is required; God's authority alone is sufficient.

journey_stops.label: Ransom for Many
journey_stops.development: Jesus declares that the Son of Man came 'to give his life as a ransom for many.' The kinsman-redeemer concept is christologically fulfilled.
journey_stops.what_changes: Redemption acquires its ultimate price. The redeemer pays not with silver or authority but with his own life.

journey_stops.label: Redeemed from the Curse
journey_stops.development: Christ 'redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us.' The exchange is explicit: he takes the curse, we receive the blessing.
journey_stops.what_changes: Redemption addresses the deepest bondage: not Egypt or Babylon but the curse of the law itself. The scope of redemption reaches its fullest extent.

journey_stops.label: Purchased by Blood
journey_stops.development: The Lamb is worthy because 'by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Redemption is revealed as universal in scope. The redeemed community encompasses every human category; the ransoming blood has unlimited reach.

title: Prophecy & Fulfillment
description: God speaking through human messengers to reveal his will and his future. Prophecy is forth-telling (proclamation) as much as foretelling (prediction). The prophetic word creates history, and history vindicates the prophetic word.

journey_stops.label: Protoevangelium
journey_stops.development: God declares to the serpent: 'He shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel.' The first prophetic promise in Scripture.
journey_stops.what_changes: The prophetic trajectory begins. A coming figure will defeat evil, though at personal cost. Redemptive history has a goal.

journey_stops.label: A Prophet Like Moses
journey_stops.development: God promises to raise up a prophet like Moses from among the people. This figure will speak God's own words with full divine authority.
journey_stops.what_changes: Prophetic office is institutionalized. The pattern is set: God communicates through appointed human mediators.

journey_stops.label: Elijah on Carmel
journey_stops.development: Elijah confronts 450 prophets of Baal. Fire falls from heaven, vindicating YHWH. 'The LORD, he is God!'
journey_stops.what_changes: The prophet becomes a public figure who confronts national apostasy. Prophetic ministry is not merely predictive but confrontational.

journey_stops.label: Immanuel Prophecy
journey_stops.development: Isaiah declares: 'The virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel.' A sign given to the house of David.
journey_stops.what_changes: Prophecy becomes explicitly messianic. The coming figure is not merely a leader but 'God with us' in the most literal sense.

journey_stops.label: Seventy Weeks
journey_stops.development: Gabriel reveals a timeline: seventy weeks are decreed for the people and the holy city. An anointed one will be 'cut off.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Prophecy acquires temporal specificity. The divine plan operates on a revealed timeline, not arbitrary timing.

journey_stops.label: Ruler from Bethlehem
journey_stops.development: From Bethlehem Ephrathah, 'too little to be among the clans of Judah,' shall come a ruler whose origin is 'from of old, from ancient days.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Prophecy pinpoints geography. The specificity of Bethlehem will become a criterion for identifying the Messiah.

journey_stops.label: All the Scriptures Concerning Himself
journey_stops.development: The risen Jesus walks with the Emmaus disciples, interpreting 'in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.' Moses and all the prophets are christological.
journey_stops.what_changes: The entire prophetic corpus is revealed as christocentric. Every prophecy finds its coherence in one person.

journey_stops.label: Testimony of Jesus Is the Spirit of Prophecy
journey_stops.development: The angel declares that 'the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy.' Prophecy's essential content and goal is Christ.
journey_stops.what_changes: The prophetic tradition receives its definitive hermeneutical key. Prophecy is not about predictions but about a person.

title: Suffering & Lament
description: The experience of pain, loss, and grief that pervades human existence. Scripture neither explains suffering away nor ignores it, but places it within the context of covenant relationship — where lament is itself an act of faith.

journey_stops.label: Curse and Toil
journey_stops.development: Pain in childbirth, conflict in marriage, thorns in the ground, death as the final horizon. Suffering enters human experience as consequence of the fall.
journey_stops.what_changes: Suffering is established as universal and connected to human rebellion. The world as experienced is not the world as originally designed.

journey_stops.label: Job's Undeserved Suffering
journey_stops.development: A blameless man loses everything. The retribution principle — the righteous prosper, the wicked suffer — is shattered.
journey_stops.what_changes: Suffering is revealed to be irreducible to simple moral calculus. The question 'Why do the righteous suffer?' is posed with full force.

journey_stops.label: My God, Why Have You Forsaken Me?
journey_stops.development: The psalmist cries out in abandonment. Surrounded by enemies, mocked, physically broken. Yet the psalm turns to praise in its second half.
journey_stops.what_changes: Suffering becomes liturgical and prophetic. Lament is given a canonical form that later generations will use, including Jesus himself.

journey_stops.label: Man of Sorrows
journey_stops.development: The Servant is 'despised and rejected, a man of sorrows.' He bears griefs, carries sorrows, is wounded for others' transgressions.
journey_stops.what_changes: Suffering becomes vicarious and redemptive. One person's suffering can accomplish atonement for many.

journey_stops.label: Hope in the Midst of Suffering
journey_stops.development: In the ashes of Jerusalem: 'The steadfast love of the LORD never ceases; his mercies never come to an end.' Hope surfaces from the deepest grief.
journey_stops.what_changes: Suffering does not have the last word. Even in the worst imaginable circumstances, divine faithfulness endures.

journey_stops.label: The Way of the Cross
journey_stops.development: Jesus begins teaching that the Son of Man must suffer, be rejected, and be killed. He calls disciples to take up their cross.
journey_stops.what_changes: Suffering becomes volitional and paradigmatic. The Messiah's suffering is not failure but divine necessity, and discipleship means following the same path.

journey_stops.label: Present Suffering, Future Glory
journey_stops.development: Paul declares that 'the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Suffering is set within an eschatological framework. Present pain is real but penultimate; glory is the final reality.

journey_stops.label: Sharing Christ's Sufferings
journey_stops.development: Believers are told not to be surprised by suffering but to 'rejoice insofar as you share Christ's sufferings.' Suffering is participation in Christ.
journey_stops.what_changes: Suffering acquires christological meaning for every believer. Union with Christ includes union in his suffering.

title: Resurrection
description: The restoration of bodily life after death. Foreshadowed in Isaac's near-sacrifice, hinted in the prophets, dramatized in Ezekiel's dry bones, and accomplished in Christ's empty tomb, resurrection is the linchpin of Christian hope.

journey_stops.label: My Redeemer Lives
journey_stops.development: Job declares: 'I know that my Redeemer lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth. In my flesh I shall see God.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The first explicit hope of bodily vindication after death appears. Even before resurrection theology develops, the seed is planted.

journey_stops.label: Death Swallowed Up
journey_stops.development: On the mountain of the LORD, God 'will swallow up death forever' and 'wipe away tears from all faces.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Death is declared temporary, not permanent. The prophetic vision includes the reversal of death itself.

journey_stops.label: Dry Bones Live
journey_stops.development: The valley of dry bones is reconstituted: bones, sinews, flesh, breath. 'I will open your graves and raise you from your graves, O my people.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Resurrection imagery is applied to national restoration, but the language transcends metaphor. Death's reversal becomes thinkable.

journey_stops.label: Many Who Sleep Shall Awake
journey_stops.development: The most explicit OT resurrection text: 'Many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Individual, bodily resurrection becomes an explicit eschatological expectation. The righteous and the wicked face different destinies.

journey_stops.label: The Empty Tomb
journey_stops.development: The women find the stone rolled away and the tomb empty. Two angels declare: 'He is not here, but has risen.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Resurrection moves from prophecy to event. The hope of centuries becomes historical reality on the third day.

journey_stops.label: The Resurrection Tradition
journey_stops.development: Paul transmits the earliest resurrection creed: Christ died, was buried, was raised on the third day, and appeared to Cephas, the Twelve, and over 500.
journey_stops.what_changes: The resurrection is documented as tradition received and transmitted. Multiple witnesses establish its historicity.

journey_stops.label: Firstfruits of Those Who Sleep
journey_stops.development: Christ's resurrection is 'the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.' As in Adam all die, so in Christ shall all be made alive.
journey_stops.what_changes: Christ's resurrection guarantees the future resurrection of all believers. His rising is not an isolated event but the beginning of a harvest.

journey_stops.label: First Resurrection
journey_stops.development: Those who had been faithful 'came to life and reigned with Christ.' The first resurrection anticipates the final transformation.
journey_stops.what_changes: Resurrection is the gateway to co-reign with Christ. The hope of bodily rising is inseparable from the hope of sharing in Christ's kingdom.

sections.title: OT Hints & Shadows
sections.content: Resurrection hope emerges slowly in Israel's Scripture. Job clings to a Redeemer who will stand on the earth after his death. The Psalmist trusts that God will not abandon the faithful to Sheol. Isaiah envisions death swallowed up forever. Ezekiel watches dry bones rattle to life. Daniel promises that many who sleep in the dust will awake. These texts were not originally read as resurrection promises — they were prayers of trust, prophetic visions of restoration, and apocalyptic expectations. But the NT authors, reading backward from the empty tomb, heard resurrection all along.

sections.title: Resurrection in the Gospels
sections.content: Each Gospel tells the resurrection with distinctive emphasis. Mark ends abruptly at the empty tomb — fear and silence, as if to say the resurrection cannot be domesticated into a tidy narrative. Matthew adds an earthquake, an angel descending like lightning, and guards struck dead — apocalyptic drama announcing the turn of the ages. Luke gives us the road to Emmaus, where the risen Jesus opens Scripture before he opens their eyes, and then a meal of broiled fish that proves his body is real. John offers the most intimate accounts: Mary Magdalene mistaking Jesus for the gardener, Thomas's confession before the wounds, and a beach breakfast that restores Peter. Together they present a resurrection that is physical yet transformed, continuous yet startlingly new.

sections.title: Paul's Resurrection Theology
sections.content: For Paul, the resurrection is not one doctrine among many — it is the foundation on which everything else stands. Remove the resurrection and the entire structure collapses: preaching is useless, faith is futile, sins are unforgiven, the dead are lost (1 Cor 15:14-18). Paul transmits the earliest resurrection creed (15:3-8), stakes his apostleship on his encounter with the risen Christ (15:8), and develops the most sophisticated resurrection theology in the NT: Christ is the firstfruits, guaranteeing a harvest; the resurrection body is sown perishable and raised imperishable; the last enemy to be destroyed is death itself. In Romans, resurrection undergirds justification (4:25), sanctification (6:4-5), and glorification (8:11). It is the power Paul longs to know (Phil 3:10) and the hope that sustains him through suffering (2 Cor 4:14).

sections.title: The Resurrection Body
sections.content: What kind of body will the raised have? Paul's answer is both illuminating and tantalizing. He uses the seed analogy: what is sown bears no resemblance to what comes up, yet there is continuity — the same life, a new form. The resurrection body is imperishable, glorious, powerful, and 'spiritual' — not meaning immaterial, but animated by the Spirit rather than by mere natural life. Jesus' risen body in the Gospels is touchable, eatable, wound-bearing, yet able to appear through locked doors and vanish at will. It belongs to the new creation — a body fit for a world where death has been abolished.

sections.title: Resurrection and New Creation
sections.content: Resurrection is not escape from the material world but its redemption. Paul envisions all creation liberated from bondage to decay (Rom 8:21). Revelation pictures not souls floating in heaven but a new heaven and new earth, a city descending, God dwelling with his people in resurrected bodies in a renewed cosmos. The resurrection of Jesus is the first installment of this cosmic renovation — the 'firstfruits' that guarantees the full harvest. In this framework, the bodily resurrection of believers is not an addendum to salvation but its consummation: the redemption of our bodies (Rom 8:23), the transformation of the perishable into the imperishable (1 Cor 15:53), and the final defeat of death itself (1 Cor 15:26).

verses.note: My Redeemer lives — earliest personal resurrection hope
You will not abandon me to the realm of the dead
God will redeem me from the realm of the dead
He will swallow up death forever
Your dead will live; their bodies will rise
Valley of dry bones — death reversed by divine breath
Multitudes who sleep in the dust will awake
On the third day he will restore us
He is not here; he has risen
He has risen! He is not here
Why do you look for the living among the dead?
The Lord has risen and has appeared to Simon
I am the resurrection and the life
Stop doubting and believe — My Lord and my God
God raised him, freeing him from the agony of death
He has given proof by raising him from the dead
Declared Son of God by his resurrection from the dead
United with him in a resurrection like his
The Spirit who raised Jesus will give life to your mortal bodies
The earliest resurrection creed
Christ, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep
Sown perishable, raised imperishable
Death has been swallowed up in victory
The power of his resurrection
The dead in Christ will rise first
A living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ
I am the Living One; I was dead, and now look, I am alive
Blessed are those who share in the first resurrection
There will be no more death

title: Law & Torah
description: God's instruction for covenant life. Torah is not mere legislation but teaching — the revelation of God's character and the path to human flourishing. The law exposes sin, guides conduct, and points forward to Christ who fulfills it.

journey_stops.label: The Ten Commandments
journey_stops.development: God speaks directly to the assembled people. The Decalogue establishes the moral foundation: duties to God (1-4) and neighbor (5-10).
journey_stops.what_changes: Divine will is codified. Israel receives not merely guidance but binding covenant stipulations from the mouth of God.

journey_stops.label: The Shema
journey_stops.development: 'Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one.' Love God with all heart, soul, and might. Teach diligently to your children.
journey_stops.what_changes: Torah observance is rooted in love, not mere obedience. The law is relational before it is regulatory.

journey_stops.label: Torah as Delight
journey_stops.development: The law is 'perfect, reviving the soul.' It is sweeter than honey, more desired than gold. Torah is not burden but gift.
journey_stops.what_changes: Torah piety is expressed as joy and desire. The psalmist celebrates the law rather than enduring it.

journey_stops.label: Oh How I Love Your Law
journey_stops.development: The longest psalm is an acrostic meditation on Torah. 'Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Torah devotion reaches its literary peak. Every letter of the alphabet is pressed into service to praise God's instruction.

journey_stops.label: Law Written on Hearts
journey_stops.development: In the new covenant, God declares: 'I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts.' External code becomes internal reality.
journey_stops.what_changes: Torah is not abolished but internalized. The problem was never the law itself but the human heart's inability to keep it.

journey_stops.label: Not to Abolish but to Fulfill
journey_stops.development: Jesus declares he came not to abolish the Law but to fulfill it. Not the smallest letter will pass away until all is accomplished.
journey_stops.what_changes: The law finds its intended goal in Christ. Fulfillment is neither abolition nor mere continuation but the realization of what Torah pointed toward.

journey_stops.label: The Law Is Holy
journey_stops.development: Paul insists: 'The law is holy, and the commandment is holy and righteous and good.' The problem is sin, not Torah.
journey_stops.what_changes: The law is defended against antinomian misreading. Torah remains good; the diagnosis of failure lies in the human condition, not the divine instruction.

journey_stops.label: The Law as Pedagogue
journey_stops.development: The law was a 'guardian' (paidagogos) until Christ came. Now that faith has come, believers are no longer under the custodian.
journey_stops.what_changes: Torah's role is revealed as preparatory and temporary in its supervisory function. Christ's arrival marks the transition from pedagogy to sonship.

title: Shepherd & Flock
description: The image of God as shepherd caring for his people. From Abel the first shepherd through David the shepherd-king to Christ the Good Shepherd, this metaphor captures divine provision, protection, and intimate knowledge of each sheep.

journey_stops.label: God Who Has Shepherded Me
journey_stops.development: Jacob blesses Joseph's sons, naming God as 'the God who has been my shepherd all my life long to this day.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The shepherd metaphor is applied to God for the first time. Divine care is described in terms of the most familiar occupation in the ancient world.

journey_stops.label: The LORD Is My Shepherd
journey_stops.development: David's psalm of complete trust: green pastures, still waters, the valley of the shadow of death, the overflowing cup, goodness and mercy.
journey_stops.what_changes: The shepherd image becomes Israel's defining expression of divine care. Every element of pastoral provision is theologized.

journey_stops.label: David Shepherds Israel
journey_stops.development: God chose David from the sheepfolds to shepherd his people. 'With upright heart he shepherded them and guided them with his skillful hand.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Human kingship is described as shepherding. The good king mirrors the divine shepherd's care for the flock.

journey_stops.label: Woe to the False Shepherds
journey_stops.development: Israel's leaders are condemned as shepherds who feed themselves instead of the flock. The sheep are scattered and devoured.
journey_stops.what_changes: The shepherd metaphor becomes a standard of judgment for leaders. Failed shepherding is covenant-breaking with the most vulnerable.

journey_stops.label: God Will Shepherd His Flock
journey_stops.development: God himself declares: 'I myself will search for my sheep... I will rescue them... I will feed them with good pasture.'
journey_stops.what_changes: God replaces failed human shepherds with himself. The divine shepherd will do what human leaders refused to do.

journey_stops.label: The Good Shepherd
journey_stops.development: Jesus declares: 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.' He knows his sheep by name.
journey_stops.what_changes: Ezekiel 34's promise of God shepherding his flock is fulfilled christologically. The good shepherd is simultaneously divine and self-sacrificing.

journey_stops.label: Shepherd and Overseer of Souls
journey_stops.development: Believers who were 'straying like sheep have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The shepherd image extends to spiritual oversight. Christ shepherds not merely bodies and communities but souls.

journey_stops.label: The Lamb Will Shepherd Them
journey_stops.development: The Lamb at the center of the throne 'will be their shepherd, and he will guide them to springs of living water.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The paradox is complete: the Lamb who was sacrificed becomes the eternal shepherd. Vulnerability and authority merge in the final vision.

title: Mission & Witness
description: God's purpose to bless all nations through his people. From Abraham's call to be a blessing to Israel as a light to the nations to the Great Commission, mission is not an appendix to Scripture but its heartbeat.

journey_stops.label: Blessed to Be a Blessing
journey_stops.development: God calls Abraham and promises: 'In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed.' Election is for mission, not privilege.
journey_stops.what_changes: The purpose of Israel's calling is defined from the outset. Blessing is centrifugal: received in order to be given.

journey_stops.label: Kingdom of Priests
journey_stops.development: Israel is called to be a 'kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' Priests mediate between God and others; Israel mediates for the world.
journey_stops.what_changes: National identity is missional. Israel exists not for its own sake but as a priestly bridge between God and the nations.

journey_stops.label: Light to the Nations
journey_stops.development: God declares: 'I will make you as a light for the nations, that my salvation may reach to the end of the earth.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Mission's scope becomes explicitly universal. The Servant's task is not merely to restore Israel but to bring salvation to the ends of the earth.

journey_stops.label: Nineveh Repents
journey_stops.development: Jonah reluctantly preaches; Nineveh repents in sackcloth and ashes. God relents from the disaster he had declared.
journey_stops.what_changes: Mission to the nations is shown to be effective, even when the messenger is reluctant. Gentile repentance is genuine and accepted.

journey_stops.label: The Great Commission
journey_stops.development: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them... teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Mission becomes the church's defining task. The Abrahamic promise of blessing to all families takes its final commissioning form.

journey_stops.label: Witnesses to the Ends of the Earth
journey_stops.development: Jesus maps the mission: 'You will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth.'
journey_stops.what_changes: Mission has a geographic and demographic trajectory: from center to periphery, from familiar to foreign, from Jew to Gentile.

journey_stops.label: Paul Turns to the Gentiles
journey_stops.development: Paul and Barnabas quote Isaiah 49:6: 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles.' The Servant's mission is taken up by the apostolic church.
journey_stops.what_changes: Isaiah's vision is enacted. The prophetic promise of universal mission becomes apostolic practice.

journey_stops.label: Every Nation, Tribe, Tongue
journey_stops.development: A great multitude from every nation stands before the throne, crying 'Salvation belongs to our God!' The mission is accomplished.
journey_stops.what_changes: The arc from Genesis 12:3 reaches its fulfillment. Every family of the earth is represented in the redeemed community.

title: Word of God
description: The creative and revelatory speech of God. By his word the heavens were made; by his word the prophets spoke; in the Word made flesh God's fullest self-disclosure arrived. Scripture is the written form of this living, active word.

journey_stops.label: God Said, 'Let There Be Light'
journey_stops.development: The first recorded speech of God. Creation happens through divine speech: God says, and reality conforms.
journey_stops.what_changes: The word of God is established as performative and creative. What God speaks, exists. Language and reality are linked at the deepest level.

journey_stops.label: Man Lives by Every Word
journey_stops.development: Moses reminds Israel: God humbled them with manna 'that he might make you know that man does not live by bread alone, but by every word from the mouth of the LORD.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The word of God is established as life-sustaining. Physical provision is secondary to the sustenance of divine speech.

journey_stops.label: The Still Small Voice
journey_stops.development: God is not in the wind, earthquake, or fire, but in the 'still small voice.' Elijah wraps his face and listens.
journey_stops.what_changes: The word of God is shown to come in unexpected modes. Divine communication need not be spectacular to be authoritative.

journey_stops.label: My Word Shall Not Return Empty
journey_stops.development: God's word goes out and accomplishes its purpose, like rain that waters the earth and makes it bring forth and sprout.
journey_stops.what_changes: The word of God is declared effective and irrevocable. It cannot fail to accomplish what it was sent to do.

journey_stops.label: Fire and Hammer
journey_stops.development: 'Is not my word like fire, declares the LORD, and like a hammer that breaks the rock in pieces?'
journey_stops.what_changes: The word of God has destructive and transformative power. It cannot be domesticated or made comfortable.

journey_stops.label: The Word Was God
journey_stops.development: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' The logos of Genesis 1 is a person.
journey_stops.what_changes: The word of God is revealed as personal and divine. The concept that began as divine speech becomes incarnate in Christ.

journey_stops.label: Living and Active
journey_stops.development: The word of God is 'living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and spirit.'
journey_stops.what_changes: The word of God penetrates and judges the inner person. It is not merely informational but transformationally incisive.

journey_stops.label: His Name Is the Word of God
journey_stops.development: The returning Christ is named 'The Word of God.' The creative word of Genesis, the prophetic word of the OT, and the incarnate Word of John 1 converge.
journey_stops.what_changes: The word of God reaches its eschatological climax. The one who spoke creation into being returns to speak the final word over all creation.
